title: One place instead of three
checkLabels.t3: The heading is still dark blue
checkLabels.t5: Not a single style attribute left
checkLabels.t7: The block is in the head
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the page instead of moving something. Bring the code back.
why.untouched: The code is exactly as it was. The three style attributes are still there.
hint2.untouched: First remove the three style attributes. The page will go black — that's expected. That's where the work starts.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed: `<style>` is closed with `</style>`. Like every other tag.
hint3.not-closed: `<style> p { color: teal; }` — the `</style>` is missing here. The browser keeps reading and turns the whole page into stylesheet.
hint4.not-closed: Check that the style block has both an opening and a closing tag.
why.no-h1: There is no heading. You deleted the `<h1>` instead of moving its colour.
hint2.no-h1: You're moving where the colour is written. You're not removing elements.
why.no-p: There is not a single paragraph left. You deleted them.
hint2.no-p: The two lines of text have to stay. Only their colour moves elsewhere.
why.h1-lost-color: The heading isn't dark blue any more. Your rule doesn't reach it.
hint2.h1-lost-color: A rule starts with a name. That name says which elements to touch. If it only says `p`, the heading isn't one of them.
hint3.h1-lost-color: `h2 { color: darkgreen; }` colours every `h2` on the page. Only those. An `h3` knows nothing about it.
hint4.h1-lost-color: You need two rules — one for `h1`, one for `p`. Or one that covers both.
why.p-lost-color: A paragraph is left without colour. The check looks at both, not the first one.
hint2.p-lost-color: The `p` rule isn't about one paragraph. It's about all of them.
hint3.p-lost-color: If you singled out the first paragraph somehow, undo it. The whole point here is that one rule catches all of them at once.
hint4.p-lost-color: One rule for `p`. Nothing more. Both paragraphs will colour themselves.
why.still-inline: At least one `style` attribute is still on a tag. The target is zero — the colour should live in one place.
hint2.still-inline: Search the code for the word `style` followed by an equals sign. There should be none.
hint3.still-inline:
You may have removed only one `

`. There were three attributes — the heading and both paragraphs.
hint4.still-inline: Delete `style="color: darkblue"` from all three tags. The rest of them stays as it is.
why.no-style-tag: There is no style block. You removed the attributes but never wrote the rule — that's why the page is black.
hint2.no-style-tag: Rules live in a `<style>` tag. It doesn't show on the page — it says how everything else should look.
hint3.no-style-tag: `<style> h2 { color: darkgreen; } </style>` — the tag is the wrapper, the rule sits inside it.
hint4.no-style-tag: Add a `<style>` inside `<head>` and write the rules in it.
why.style-in-body: The style block is outside `<head>`. It works — and that's exactly what makes it a trap.
hint2.style-in-body: An HTML validator will flag it as an error. By the spec, `<style>` belongs in the head, with no exceptions.
hint3.style-in-body: The proposal to allow it in the body was rejected by the people who write the browsers. Their argument: the page is already painted, the styles arrive after, and the browser has to recompute layout and paint it all again.
hint4.style-in-body: Move the whole `<style>` block between `<head>` and `</head>`.
why.lost-p: A paragraph is gone. You're moving the colour, not deleting text.
hint2.lost-p: Both sentences under the heading should be there.
blocks.1.text: Last lesson you wrote the same thing three times. Three tags, three times `color: darkblue`.
blocks.2.text: Now the cafe owner says she wants green. Count how many places you'd edit. Three — on a page with three elements. A real site has two hundred. And tomorrow she'll change her mind again.
blocks.5.text: `p` is a *selector* — it says which elements. Inside the curly braces is the same thing you wrote in the tag: property, colon, value. A semicolon at the end.
blocks.6.text: The difference is that this rule catches ALL paragraphs at once. One, ten, two hundred — it doesn't matter. Change the colour here and it changes everywhere.
blocks.8.text: `<head>` is information about the page. `<body>` is what people see. Styles aren't content — they're instructions on how content should look. That's why they belong in the head, and it isn't a matter of taste: by the spec, `<style>` has no right to be anywhere else.
blocks.9.text: Put them in the body and it will work. Every browser runs them. But the page is already painted when the styles arrive — so the browser recomputes the layout and paints it again. Someone on a slow connection sees black text, then repainted text.
blocks.11.text: Remove the three `style` attributes. Do the same job with rules in `<head>` — the heading and both paragraphs stay dark blue. The result is exactly the same as lesson 01. The code isn't.
blocks.14.items.0: The selector says which elements. One rule catches every element of that kind at once.
blocks.14.items.1: A rule: selector, curly braces, inside them property, colon, value, semicolon.
blocks.14.items.2: `<style>` goes in `<head>`. It works in the body, but it breaks the spec and makes the browser paint twice.
blocks.14.items.3: `style` on the tag isn't forbidden. It's just almost never the right choice.
blocks.15.text: One place for three tags is good. But that place is inside the page — a site with twenty pages needs twenty copies of the same block. So there's a third place: a whole separate file. We look at it in lesson 04.
walkthrough.0.text: Two rules, one under the other, in a single `<style>` block. One would do as well: `h1, p { color: darkblue; }` — the comma between the two selectors means both of them. It's shorter and it passes the checks too.
walkthrough.1.text: The semicolon after `darkblue` closes the declaration. With a single declaration the browser accepts it without one. Write it anyway — the day you add a second line below and forget the semicolon above comes for everyone. Lesson 54 is about exactly that day.
walkthrough.2.text: The blank line between the two rules means nothing to the browser. Whitespace and newlines are ignored everywhere in CSS except inside quotes. It's there for you.
walkthrough.3.text: I removed the attributes entirely rather than emptying them. `style=""` passes the colour check but leaves a dead attribute in the code. Don't leave traces of what you removed.
